headline: Hollywood IS vertical. Netflix + Amazon + Disney confirmed the trifecta this fortnight. LA in 6 days — the conversations just changed.
whatChanged: This run covers the week of May 25, picking up where the May 23 brief left off. The defining development: the three largest US streaming companies all now have vertical video infrastructure. Netflix launched 'Clips' on April 30 in 9 markets, Amazon Prime Video launched its 'Clips' vertical feed on May 8, and Disney+ has been running vertical ('Locker Diaries') since February. Industry observers confirmed in the week of May 4-10 that vertical is no longer an experiment — it is standard premium-streaming infrastructure. This is bigger than the Peacock licensing deal from last week, because all three together is the entire Hollywood establishment. Tim's LA meetings (Jun 1-4) are now set against this backdrop. Every Microdrama-in-a-Box B2B conversation now has three additional proof points from the world's largest streamers. Two critical awards corrections: (1) StreamTV Awards — finalists announced May 12, the 2026 window is closed; the Jun 15 deadline in state was incorrect. (2) Campaign Asia 40 Under 40 — actual deadline was April 17, 2026 (not June 30); window is closed. MARKETING-INTERACTIVE Marketing Excellence Awards SG deadline corrected to June 12 (was listed as June 5) — 17 days out, still actionable. New intelligence this run: Issa Rae's 'Screen Time' on PineDrama hit 100M views (prestige Hollywood creator formally in microdrama); Marc Jacobs launched a luxury microdrama with Rachel Sennott (brand spectrum now spans FMCG to luxury fashion — seven confirmed brands); Park Min-Kyu (Single's Inferno, Netflix) cast in FlareFlow original 'One Year Love' (Vertical 2.0 casting); LA Vertical Drama Market held second edition May 7-10 at FAB Factory Hollywood with Apollo Awards (IAVA Gala — new award category for tracking); 'Clearly me' second AI consent violation in Chinese microdrama production (Category 22 — NEM prep). Categories that still produced nothing due to network policy: app-store chart rankings, Mandarin reputation watch (Weibo/Baidu), Reddit qualitative. RSS blocked 22/22 — environment-level fix remains the only unblocker.

topOpportunities.title: Shoot trifecta piece-to-camera — 'Netflix. Amazon. Disney. Now what?' (this weekend, May 30-31)
topOpportunities.why: Netflix, Amazon, and Disney all launched vertical video feeds in the same fortnight — the entire Hollywood establishment has now signalled vertical as infrastructure. This is the single most important validation moment in Tim's public narrative to date, and it needs an on-camera reaction before he lands in LA. Every LA conversation starts from this frame. Draft at content.json (id: cn_trifecta_ptc_2605). **~25 min: 15 to record (3–4 takes), 5 to select, 5 to caption + schedule. Shoot Saturday or Sunday May 30-31.**
topOpportunities.source: Netflix Clips: https://variety.com/2026/digital/news/netflix-new-mobile-app-vertical-video-feed-clips-1236734258/ | Amazon Clips: https://www.advanced-television.com/2026/05/11/prime-video-rolling-out-clips-vertical-feed/ | Disney+: https://deadline.com/2026/02/disney-locker-diaries-vertical-drama-zombies-characters-1236724682/ Intelligence item: int_trifecta_vertical_2605

topOpportunities.title: Send Matthew Belloni / The Town pitch before Tim boards for LA (target: Wednesday May 27)
topOpportunities.why: The trifecta news makes the Belloni pitch even stronger. Lead: 'Netflix, Amazon, Disney all launched vertical feeds this month. Peacock licensed ReelShort last week. You covered DramaBox (consumer). The story you haven't told is who built the supply chain all of these platforms are now buying from — that's COL.' Send before Tim lands in LA so Belloni has time to Google Tim and find the video. **~30 min: Stuart drafts Wednesday, Tim reviews and sends. Target: Wednesday May 27 or Thursday May 28 at latest.**
topOpportunities.source: data/state/media.json — seed-the-town-with-matthew-belloni. Intelligence item int_trifecta_vertical_2605. Contact: matthew@puck.news

topOpportunities.title: MARKETING-INTERACTIVE Marketing Excellence Awards SG — deadline June 12 (17 days)
topOpportunities.why: Best Content Marketing Strategy category. June 12 deadline, 17 days out. Eligibility period: March 2025 to April 2026 — COL's vertical distribution campaign, the Peacock licensing deal, and the Microdrama-in-a-Box launch all fall within scope. Previous deadline entry (June 5) was incorrect — there is more time than previously thought. This is still Tim's best near-term Singapore-market award. **~90 min total: 60 min to draft responses + evidence, 30 min to file. Fee: ~USD 270. Stuart leads the draft, Tim reviews.**

topOpportunities.title: DM Maria Rua Aguete — NEM pre-panel sync on trifecta data and panel framing
topOpportunities.why: NEM Dubrovnik panel (Jun 8-11) is 13 days out. With Netflix/Amazon/Disney all in vertical this week, Tim and Maria need to agree on: (1) which market sizing number leads on panel ($11B Omdia preferred), (2) how the trifecta changes the panel framing ('risky business' framing is now harder to argue — Tim can open by naming the trifecta), (3) data alignment on Australia 20% proof point. **Watch — send a WhatsApp or LinkedIn DM by Thursday May 28. ~5 min.**

topOpportunities.title: IAVA Apollo Awards — track for next cycle; meet LAVDM organizers during LA Jun 1-4
topOpportunities.why: The LA Vertical Drama Market (LAVDM) held its second edition May 7-10 at FAB Factory Hollywood, with Apollo Awards (IAVA Gala, 15 categories: acting, directing, writing, art design, technology). The event runs semi-annually — next edition likely Sep 2026. This is the Hollywood home for the vertical drama category. LA trip Jun 1-4 is an opportunity to meet the LAVDM organizers in person, discuss Sep speaking slot, and flag IAVA Apollo Awards as a new award category for FlareFlow originals. **Watch — no formal action yet, just note for LA meeting calendar.**

bestMediaAngle.angle: The supply chain story behind the streaming trifecta: 'Netflix, Amazon, and Disney all launched vertical feeds this month. Peacock licensed ReelShort last week. Every major US streamer is now building vertical infrastructure — but none of them built the content supply chain. That story is COL.' Tim has the receipts: 1,700 titles, USD 1M+ B2B, $2M TikTok Q1, Peacock licensing, Park Min-Kyu, Microdrama-in-a-Box.
bestMediaAngle.why: The trifecta is the news peg that makes this a breaking story, not a profile. Belloni's audience (US entertainment-business decision-makers) is exactly who needs to understand the supply-chain question the trifecta creates. One email → Puck feature + The Town episode. Tim should be able to take a call during LA Jun 1-4 if pitch lands this week.
bestMediaAngle.evidence: Netflix Clips (Variety Apr 30), Amazon Prime Clips (Advanced Television May 11), Disney+ Locker Diaries (Deadline Feb 2026), Peacock/ReelShort (C21Media May 11). Tim's B2B receipts: Deadline/Variety May 2026. Date checked: 2026-05-26.

bestLinkedInAngle.hook: Netflix. Amazon. Disney. All vertical. Now what?
bestLinkedInAngle.angle: Piece-to-camera, ~60 seconds, phone-shot, no edit. Tim direct to camera outdoors, no jacket. Hook: three names, three seconds. Beat structure: name the trifecta → 'Three is a supply-chain decision' → 'I'm in LA next week. Guess what we're talking about.' Forward close. Full script and shoot instructions in content.json (id: cn_trifecta_ptc_2605). Shoot Saturday or Sunday May 30-31.
bestLinkedInAngle.why: The trifecta is the clearest validation moment in vertical drama history — bigger than Peacock because it is the entire Hollywood establishment. Reacting on camera (not in writing) is the Category 21 imperative for a category that IS mobile-first video. Tim in LA in 6 days makes the forward close feel live and urgent.

bestCommentOpportunity.targetAuthor: Patrick Frater or Todd Spangler (Variety), or Lauren Forristal (TechCrunch), or anyone in Tim's LinkedIn feed posting about Netflix/Amazon/Disney vertical feeds
bestCommentOpportunity.suggestedComment: Three streamers in 30 days isn't a coincidence. Netflix Clips, Amazon Clips, Disney+ vertical — that's every major US subscription platform signalling that portrait mode is now standard streaming infrastructure, not an experiment. The interesting question this creates isn't 'is vertical real?' — that conversation ended this week. The question now is who has the content supply chain that connects these platforms to the shows they need. That's where the real race starts.
bestCommentOpportunity.why: Tim commenting on the trifecta story positions him as the operator who can explain the supply-chain dimension that consumer-focused coverage misses. Short, specific, operator POV — not a retweet. Every LinkedIn connection who sees the comment gets the COL context from Tim's profile.
bestCommentOpportunity.risk: Low. Keep it under 150 words. One point, one implication. Do not mention COL by name in the comment — profile does that work. Do not be triumphalist.

bestRelationshipMove.name: Matthew Belloni
bestRelationshipMove.whyMatters: The trifecta plus Peacock together is a genuine news moment for Belloni's audience. One cold email this week → potential podcast appearance during or after LA trip. This is the highest-ROI single outreach in Tim's pipeline.
bestRelationshipMove.timRelevance: Subject line writes itself: 'The supply chain story behind the Netflix/Amazon/Disney vertical week.' Body: 3 stats, 1 frame, 1 ask. Shoot the trifecta piece-to-camera first so Belloni can watch it when he Googles Tim.
bestRelationshipMove.colRelevance: Tier-1 US entertainment press. Puck feature + The Town appearance = the narrative control COL needs in the Hollywood conversation.
bestRelationshipMove.bestApproach: Cold email matthew@puck.news. Subject: 'The supply chain story behind the Netflix/Amazon/Disney vertical week.' 3 stats: 1,700 titles, $2M TikTok Q1, Peacock licensed ReelShort. 1 frame: 'COL built the rails before the streamers needed them.' 1 ask: '15-min call, I'm in LA June 1-4.'
bestRelationshipMove.privateMove: Stuart drafts Wednesday May 27. Tim sends Thursday May 28. Pitch goes after trifecta piece-to-camera is live.
bestRelationshipMove.risk: Generic pitch energy. First sentence must be the news peg — 'Netflix, Amazon, Disney all went vertical this week' — not Tim's credentials.

bestColOpportunity.title: LA streaming meetings: position COL/Microdrama-in-a-Box as the supply-chain standard for every streamer now building vertical infrastructure
bestColOpportunity.why: Peacock validated the licensing model last week. Netflix, Amazon, and Disney launching vertical feeds this week means every major US streamer is now facing the same question: where does the content come from? LA trip Jun 1-4, with Jason Ander (new Head of US Partnerships, ex-Paramount/Hulu) in the room, is the window to position COL as the infrastructure answer — before competitors do.
bestColOpportunity.commercialUpside: A content licensing or white-label deal with any single additional major US streamer (Netflix, Amazon, Disney+, Max, Paramount+) would be: multi-million-dollar recurring revenue, tier-1 PR moment ('second major US streamer after Peacock chooses COL'), and a step-change in Microdrama-in-a-Box B2B credibility. Even a 'watch list' meeting with a SVP+ at any of the three is commercially significant.
bestColOpportunity.nextStep: Stuart + Tim build a single-page LA brief by Thursday May 29: 'From Peacock to the trifecta — what the supply-chain conversation looks like in 2026.' Tim brings it to every LA meeting Jun 1-4. Jason Ander coordinates studio contact list. Target: SVP+ content at Netflix Mobile, Amazon Prime Video originals, Disney+ digital, Max, Paramount+.

risingTrend.trend: Every major US streaming platform now has a vertical video product. The format question is settled. The infrastructure race has begun.
risingTrend.evidence: Netflix Clips: 9 markets, launched April 30 (Variety). Amazon Prime Video Clips: launched May 8 (Advanced Television). Disney+ Locker Diaries: February 2026 (Deadline). Peacock/ReelShort: May 11 (C21Media). Four of the five major US streamers now have a vertical vertical product or catalogue deal in place. Fifth (Max/Paramount+) has announced interest. Date checked: 2026-05-26.
risingTrend.implication: Tim's messaging should shift from 'explaining vertical drama to Hollywood' to 'explaining who holds the infrastructure Hollywood now needs.' The 12-month 40K goal is now achievable not as a niche operator but as a necessary counterpart to US streaming's vertical build-out. COL is the Reuters of vertical content supply.

thingToIgnore.item: StreamTV Awards 2026 — and Campaign Asia 40 Under 40 2026. Both windows are closed. StreamTV finalists were announced May 12; Campaign Asia 40U40 deadline was April 17. Neither can be acted on this cycle.
thingToIgnore.why: A brief that recommends 'submit to StreamTV Awards' when the window closed two weeks ago loses credibility. Same for 40U40. Both are removed from the 'Act on this week' surface. StreamTV and a new 'vertical mogul' award category (IAVA Apollo Awards) go into the 2027 planning cycle. Campaign Asia 40U40 — Tim has aged out for next year (will be 41). Replace with Campaign Asia Media Awards (Media Leader of the Year, deadline July 14) as the next personal awards priority.

reputationRisk.risk: Two critical framings are now sharpening as Tim enters LA and NEM: (1) The 'Chinese soft power' frame from the Yale Journal is getting more traction as the US vertical market grows — Hollywood meetings will surface it. (2) AI consent violations ('Clearly me' on May 2, 'The Peach Blossom Hairpin' earlier) are becoming a systemic story. Both will be asked in LA and at NEM. Without prepared responses, Tim either dismisses legitimate criticism (undermining his 'I say it as it is' brand) or over-defends (looking scared).
reputationRisk.mitigation: Finalize two one-liners by Thursday May 29. On soft power: 'COL builds platforms that serve local audiences with global IP. We're not exporting ideology — we're exporting infrastructure. The difference is who controls the algorithm and the monetisation.' On AI consent: 'These cases describe the floor of the market. FlareFlow's 180-title slate runs on guild contracts and production standards. The difference between the top decile and the bottom is editorial discipline — not just AI capability.' Stuart builds the full LA Q&A brief by Thursday May 29.

suggestedAction.action: This weekend (May 30-31): Help Tim record the trifecta piece-to-camera (content.json: cn_trifecta_ptc_2605) — 25 min total, highest leverage piece this cycle. Also record the NEM pre-panel piece-to-camera (cn_nem_panel_ptc_2605) — shoot Thursday/Friday May 28-29, post Friday evening so it's live during LA. Wednesday May 27: Draft Belloni pitch letter. Tim sends Thursday May 28 before landing in LA. Thursday May 28: Send Maria Rua Aguete WhatsApp re NEM pre-panel sync. Thursday May 29: Build LA Q&A brief for Tim (soft power + AI consent framings). Thu May 29: Begin MARKETING-INTERACTIVE Marketing Excellence Awards SG draft — deadline June 12. By Friday May 30: LAVDM organizer contact identified, coffee meeting requested for Jun 1 or 2. During LA (Jun 1-4): help Tim post daily vlog beats (beat sheet at content.json: cn1lpgxh9v8k3p9q).

headline: Hollywood just said yes. Shoot the reaction this weekend. Everything else is noise.
whatChanged: This is the first real intelligence sweep of the week — the two previous runs (both today, both logged as low-value) were code-verification passes, not actual runs. Today's sweep via web search (RSS still fully blocked, 22/22 denied) produced 13 reviewed intelligence items. The week's defining signal: Peacock licensed a ReelShort content package for its mobile app, launching summer 2026 — making Peacock the first major US streamer to formally treat a microdrama-native platform as a content supplier. Tim has been predicting this exact inflection point since MIP London 2026. The moment to react on camera is this weekend, before the news cycle moves on. Second major signal: COL announced the hires of Jason Ander (ex-Paramount, Hulu) as Head of US Partnerships and Eileen Low (ex-Disney) as Head of Asia Partnerships, alongside Tim's formal appointment as FlareFlow CMO — covered in Deadline and Variety this week. Third: China's major platforms (Douyin, WeChat, Taobao, Kuaishou) conducted a coordinated crackdown in May, removing 500+ microdramas for content violations — validates Tim's 'Built to Last' framing ahead of the NEM Dubrovnik panel. Fourth: P&G Studios launched 'The Golden Pear Affair', a 50-episode scripted microdrama for the Native brand — the most documented FMCG brand entry yet, adding Anna Saalfeld (P&G Studios) as a category-defining relationship target. Fifth: FlareFlow's $2M TikTok revenue in Q1 2026 is now a public proof point (per Deadline) — add to every pitch. Sixth: ATxEnterprise Singapore (May 20-22) was a confirmed Tim speaking event not previously in state — added to speaking.json. CORRECTION: Cannes Lions 2026 submission deadline was April 9, 2026 — already passed. The weekly focus entry 'Close Cannes Lions submission' is now moot; settings.json updated to remove it. Categories that still produced nothing this run: app-store chart intelligence (need network for Sensor Tower), Mandarin reputation watch (Weibo/Baidu — need network), Reddit qualitative (r/microdrama — need network). All three require environment-level network policy fix.

topOpportunities.title: Shoot Peacock/ReelShort reaction piece-to-camera — 'Hollywood just licensed vertical drama.'
topOpportunities.why: Peacock licensing ReelShort content (announced May 11) is the exact commercial trajectory Tim has been predicting publicly since MIP London. The window to react with authority closes this weekend. Every day he waits, someone else owns the frame. topPeople.privateMove: Tim briefs Jason on LA target list by Thu May 28. Jason brings his Paramount/Hulu contact list.

topPeople.name: Janine Stein
topPeople.whyMatters: Most consistent APAC media amplifier. The Peacock/ReelShort deal + COL new hires + China crackdown is a three-story cycle that ContentAsia should cover. Give Janine the exclusive angle: 'How COL's infrastructure play looks from inside the Peacock deal week.'
topPeople.timRelevance: Keeps Tim in regular APAC trade coverage between the LA trip and NEM Dubrovnik. ContentAsia's audience IS the Asian content-business decision-maker layer COL needs visible to.
topPeople.colRelevance: COL announcement coverage (hires + Peacock validation) with a ContentAsia exclusive frame.
topPeople.bestApproach: Email + LinkedIn DM. Offer: 'Janine — big week. Three things happened. I can give you the inside read.' Her audience wants the distribution infrastructure story.
topPeople.privateMove: Email by Wednesday May 27. After Tim's Peacock piece-to-camera posts.

bestMediaAngle.angle: The supply chain story behind the Peacock/ReelShort deal: 'You covered DramaBox (consumer). Now Peacock has licensed ReelShort (supply chain). The story nobody has told yet is who built the rails that Peacock just started buying — and what that means for every other major streamer watching.' Tim has the receipts: 1,700 titles, USD 1M+ B2B, $2M TikTok Q1, Microdrama-in-a-Box.
bestMediaAngle.why: Peacock/ReelShort is the news peg that makes the pitch a news story, not a profile. Belloni's audience (US entertainment-business decision-makers) is exactly who needs to understand the supply chain, not just the format. One email → Puck feature + The Town episode.
bestMediaAngle.evidence: Peacock/ReelShort deal confirmed via C21Media, Deadline, TechCrunch, Peacock official (May 2026). Tim's B2B receipts publicly documented in Deadline/Variety this week. Date checked: 2026-05-23.

bestLinkedInAngle.angle: Piece-to-camera, 60 seconds, phone-shot, no edit. Tim direct to camera, outdoors, no jacket. Hook in 5 words ('Hollywood just licensed vertical drama.'). Three-beat time progression: '2 years ago / last year / this week — they stopped asking.' Forward close: 'I'm in LA next week. Guess what we're talking about.' This is the 'I told you so' moment done with operator confidence. Full script and beat sheet in content.json (id: cn_peacock_reaction_2605). Shoot Sat or Sun May 23-24.
bestLinkedInAngle.why: The Peacock/ReelShort deal is the clearest validation moment in Tim's public narrative to date. Reacting on camera (not in writing) is the Category 21 imperative — and it's also the most native form in a category that IS mobile-first video.

bestCommentOpportunity.targetAuthor: Lauren Forristal / TechCrunch — or any trade journalist who files a Peacock/ReelShort piece this week
bestCommentOpportunity.suggestedComment: The really interesting detail here isn't that Peacock is adding vertical video. It's that they're licensing from ReelShort — a microdrama-native platform — rather than commissioning in-house. That's a supply-chain decision. Peacock just told the market: there are specialists in vertical production who are better at this than a major streamer building from scratch. The next question is who else is watching that decision.
bestCommentOpportunity.why: Tim commenting on the TechCrunch or C21Media Peacock/ReelShort piece positions him as the operator who can explain the B2B dimension that the consumer-focused trade coverage misses. Short, specific, operator POV — not a retweet.
bestCommentOpportunity.risk: Low. The only risk is over-length — keep it under 150 words. One point, one implication. Do not mention COL by name in the comment (let Tim's profile do that work).

bestRelationshipMove.name: Anna Saalfeld
bestRelationshipMove.whyMatters: P&G's 'The Golden Pear Affair' is the most documented FMCG microdrama entry yet. Saalfeld is the architect. This is the relationship that turns Tim from 'person who predicted brand entry' to 'person P&G calls when they want to scale'.
bestRelationshipMove.timRelevance: Tim's brand-entry thesis now has a named P&G lead. DM opens the COL-to-FMCG pipeline at the most valuable possible entry point.
bestRelationshipMove.colRelevance: P&G is the world's largest advertiser. A COL partnership conversation — even a 15-min call — is a tier-1 commercial signal.
bestRelationshipMove.bestApproach: LinkedIn DM after Tim's Peacock piece-to-camera posts this weekend. Message: 'Saw what you built with Native — the soap-opera heritage reframe is exactly right. If you're thinking about what this looks like at scale, happy to share what the distribution side looks like from inside the largest vertical catalogue.' No pitch. One observation, one offer.
bestRelationshipMove.publicMove: Tim reposts the Marketing Dive P&G piece with a one-liner: 'P&G just proved what I've been saying for two years. Brands didn't adopt vertical — they came home to the format that always worked, just on the right screen.'
bestRelationshipMove.privateMove: Stuart finds warm intro vector via Campaign Asia or Cannes Lions connections. DM goes after piece-to-camera posts (May 23-24 target).
bestRelationshipMove.risk: Approaching as a vendor. Tim should lead as a peer — creative operator to creative operator. No COL product pitch in the opening DM.

bestColOpportunity.title: Peacock validation opens the door: pitch Microdrama-in-a-Box to the other major US streamers (Disney+, Max, Paramount+) before they replicate what Peacock just did in-house
bestColOpportunity.why: Peacock just demonstrated the model: license content from a vertical-native platform rather than build in-house. Every other major US streamer's content team is now having an internal meeting about this. The window to position COL/Microdrama-in-a-Box as the infrastructure layer — not just the content supplier — is open now. LA trip Jun 1–4 is the in-person window.
bestColOpportunity.commercialUpside: A white-label or content licensing deal with one additional major US streamer: multi-million-dollar recurring revenue, tier-1 PR moment, strengthens the COL narrative as the supply-chain standard. Even a 'watch list' meeting with Disney+ or Max is commercially significant.
bestColOpportunity.nextStep: Stuart + Tim build a one-page COL infrastructure brief specifically for US streamers by Wed May 27 — framing the Peacock deal as proof of model, and positioning COL as the platform that can replicate it for other streamers at scale. Tim brings to LA Jun 1 meetings. Target: SVP+ at Disney+ US, Max, Paramount+ and a Peacock follow-up.

risingTrend.trend: Brand advertisers are making vertical drama a formal budget line item. The 'brands are coming' prediction is now a documented fact: P&G, Unilever, Crocs, Shopee, Starbucks, Maybelline — six brands with confirmed vertical campaigns in 2025-2026.
risingTrend.evidence: P&G 'The Golden Pear Affair' (Marketing Dive, May 2026) — 50-episode series, Dentsu Entertainment production. Starbucks and Maybelline confirmed via Marketing Brew (2026). Crocs and Shopee per STRATEGIC_PROFILE.md. Date checked: 2026-05-23.
risingTrend.implication: Tim's positioning should drift from 'microdrama operator explaining the format to Hollywood' to 'vertical infrastructure provider for brands who've already decided to enter the format.' The LA trip and NEM Dubrovnik both now include brand-CMO conversations as equally important as studio-exec conversations.

thingToIgnore.item: Cannes Lions 2026 — the submission deadline was April 9, 2026. It has passed. The weekly focus entry 'Close Cannes Lions submission' is moot. Stuart should update settings.json to remove it and replace it with a Cannes Lions 2027 tracking note (PanelPicker and submission windows open Jan 2027). No action required or possible this cycle.
thingToIgnore.why: A team item that's already closed creates false urgency and dilutes focus on what's actually actionable. The three actionable items this week (Peacock piece-to-camera, StreamTV Awards, pre-LA piece-to-camera) are the right 3. Adding a fourth that can't be executed = the brief loses authority.

reputationRisk.risk: Two critical framings are live this week and will surface at NEM Dubrovnik and LA meetings: (1) Yale Journal 'micro-drama as soft power' — framing COL/ReelShort as Chinese cultural infrastructure, which could trigger US regulatory concern or Hollywood reticence; (2) The Wrap 'dark underbelly' — labour rates, AI consent violations, racial homogeneity of US microdrama productions. Tim will be asked about both in LA and at NEM. Without prepared responses, he risks either dismissing legitimate criticisms (undermining his 'I say it as it is' brand) or over-defending (looking scared).
reputationRisk.mitigation: Prepare two sharp one-liners this week. On soft power: 'COL builds platforms that serve local audiences with global IP. We're not exporting ideology — we're exporting infrastructure. There's a difference.' On The Wrap: 'They're describing the floor of the market. We're building the ceiling — 180 FlareFlow originals in 2026, $45M investment, productions in Singapore, the US, and with talent from 10+ territories.' Stuart builds the Q&A brief for Tim's LA meetings by Thu May 29.

suggestedAction.action: This weekend (May 23-24): help Tim record + post the Peacock reaction piece-to-camera (content.json: cn_peacock_reaction_2605) — ~25 min total, highest leverage piece this cycle. By Mon May 26: draft Belloni pitch with Peacock news peg, send Tue May 27. By Tue May 27: begin StreamTV Awards submission assembly — Jun 15 deadline, Tim has all the receipts, ~90 min to file. Update settings.json: remove 'Close Cannes Lions submission' from weeklyFocus (deadline passed April 9). By Thu May 29: build LA crisis Q&A brief for Tim (soft power + ethics framings). Pre-LA piece-to-camera (already drafted): shoot + post Sat May 30.
